Adiabatic Hybrid Cooling is Compatible with NVIDIA’s new Closed Loop System


Nvidia, $NVDA, has announced a warm-water cooling system that it says can dramatically reduce the amount of water a data center uses, eliminating “pretty much all water usage” inside the data center.


  • Unusual Whales reports Nvidia's new 45°C closed-loop liquid cooling system for AI data centers, using a water-glycol mix recirculated without evaporative cooling to achieve near-zero on-site water consumption.
  • The design eliminates most chiller needs in favorable climates via dry coolers, cutting facility water use by up to 100% from traditional systems and saving significant power and costs for large-scale operations.
  • This addresses a key barrier to data center buildouts amid AI-driven demand, where water scarcity has stalled projects, though it does not cover indirect water use in electricity generation.



list that compatible US climates and US States that this would work

Thought for 1m 5s
Nvidia's 45°C (113°F) warm-water closed-loop liquid cooling system (for its Rubin platform and similar AI infrastructure) enables dry coolers (large air-to-liquid heat exchangers, like giant radiators) to reject heat from the coolant loop using ambient air. This dramatically cuts or eliminates on-site water usage (near zero vs. millions of gallons per MW/year with traditional evaporative cooling towers) by avoiding evaporation in a sealed loop.
It works across most US states and climates, but performance is best (often chiller-free or with minimal "trim" chillers only on peak hot days) in cooler areas. Hotter climates still benefit greatly from major water and energy savings via hybrids, though dry coolers need larger sizing or supplemental mechanical cooling on the hottest days.

Favorable US Climates

Best results occur in cooler to cold climates where ambient dry-bulb temperatures frequently allow efficient heat rejection (typically with a 5–10°C+ approach temperature to the 45°C coolant). These include:
  • Cool marine (e.g., Pacific Northwest)
  • Cool humid/dry continental
  • Cold humid/dry
  • Very cold / subarctic
These align with ASHRAE/IECC climate zones roughly 5C, 6, 7, and 8 (and cooler parts of zone 5/4). Hot-dry or hot-humid zones (1–3) work with hybrids but rely more on trim chillers during summer peaks.

Highly Compatible US States (Excellent Dry Cooler Performance)

These states offer the strongest year-round or near-year-round benefits with minimal or no chiller use in most conditions:
  • Washington (especially western)
  • Oregon
  • Idaho
  • Montana
  • Wyoming
  • North Dakota
  • South Dakota
  • Minnesota
  • Wisconsin
  • Michigan (particularly Upper Peninsula)
  • Vermont, New Hampshire, Maine
  • Alaska
  • Upstate New York and northern Pennsylvania

Moderately Compatible States (Strong Hybrid Performance)

Dry coolers handle most of the load; trim chillers engage on the hottest days/periods:
  • Colorado (higher elevations best)
  • Utah
  • California (northern, coastal, and mountainous areas; less ideal in hot Central Valley or Southern deserts)
  • Ohio
  • Illinois (northern areas)
  • Broader New York and Pennsylvania
  • Parts of Indiana and similar Midwest/northern states

Viable Nationwide (with Hybrids; Major Water Savings)

The system works in all US states, including hotter ones, thanks to the higher 45°C coolant temperature enabling better dry cooler efficiency than older lower-temp systems. Examples:
  • Texas, Arizona, Nevada, New Mexico — Dry coolers sized for peaks + trim chillers on extreme days; still delivers huge water reductions vs. towers.
  • Florida, Georgia, Southeast states — Hybrid setups effective; closed-loop minimizes water use despite humidity.
  • Other hot/mixed areas (e.g., parts of California deserts or Oklahoma).
In very hot spots like Phoenix, mechanical cooling may run only a small percentage of the year (sometimes cited as low as ~1% in optimistic scenarios).

Bottom line: This technology is broadly compatible across the US and maximizes sustainability (water + energy) in cooler northern, northwestern, and mountainous states. It still provides significant advantages everywhere via closed-loop dry or hybrid cooling. Site-specific engineering (dry cooler sizing, hybrid controls, and local weather data) determines exact performance. For precise project planning, consult ASHRAE climate data and cooling system vendors.
